name: Cypress Death
description: Cypress Death, referring to the Mediterranean cypress tree (Cupressus sempervirens) and its association with mortality, mourning, and the underworld, holds a significant place in Mediterranean mythology and funerary practices. Its evergreen nature, paradoxically symbolizing both immortality and grief, made it a prevalent symbol of remembrance. The tree's dark, slender form, reaching towards the heavens, was seen as a pathway for souls ascending to the afterlife. In ancient Greece and Rome, cypress was closely linked to deities of the underworld, particularly Hades/Pluto. It was commonly planted in cemeteries and used in funeral processions, signifying respect for the deceased and hope for their eternal rest. Burning cypress branches was believed to purify the air and protect against evil spirits during mourning rituals. The wood was sometimes used for sarcophagi, further reinforcing its connection to death and the afterlife. Beyond funerary uses, cypress was also associated with resilience and strength, reflecting the enduring nature of memory and the human spirit's ability to overcome grief. Cypress cones and leaves were occasionally used in medicinal preparations, though its primary role was symbolic rather than practical. Its presence in art, literature, and folklore solidifies its status as a potent emblem of mortality and remembrance in Mediterranean cultures.
subtitle: Symbol of death, mourning, and immortality

keyMyths.title: The Cypress and the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Cypress trees were believed to be gateways to the underworld, planted near tombs to guide souls. Their evergreen nature symbolized eternal life beyond death, offering solace to mourners.

sources.author: Robert Graves
sources.description: Provides context on the association of cypress with Hades and funerary rites.
sources.text: Provides context on the association of cypress with Hades and funerary rites.

sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Details the practical and symbolic uses of cypress in ancient Rome.
